blocks.alt: Functions are reusable blocks of code

blocks.text: A function is a named, reusable block of code. You define it once and call it by name whenever you need it. Without functions, you copy and paste the same logic everywhere — and when you need to change it, you change it in every place.

blocks.desc: Define a block of code named 'greetPlayer' — does not run yet
Run that block of code right now

content.heading: Example of creating & calling a function

blocks.text: Here's a simple function that logs a greeting message. Notice how the function definition does not run the code inside it. Only when we call greetPlayer() does it execute.

lines: // This is the body of the function — code that runs when you call it

blocks.label: Why use functions?
blocks.text: Functions let you reuse code without copying and pasting. They also help organize your code into logical chunks with clear names.

lines: console.log('This runs every time showMessage is called.');
// Writing showMessage without () does NOT call it

blocks.label: Defining vs calling
blocks.text: Writing function showMessage() {} only creates the function — it does not run it. Writing showMessage() runs it. The parentheses are what trigger execution.

exercises.title: What does a function do?
tasks: What does writing function greetPlayer() {} do?
exercises.hint: Think about what happens when you define a function — does it run right away?

options.label: Creates a reusable block of code called greetPlayer

exercises.explanation: Defining a function only creates it — it does not run it. Think of it like writing a recipe. Writing the recipe does not cook the food. You have to call the function separately to make it run.

exercises.title: Spot the correct definition
tasks: Which of these correctly defines a function called sayHello?
exercises.hint: A function definition always starts with the word 'function', then the name, then ().
exercises.explanation: A function definition has four parts: the word 'function', the name, parentheses (), and curly braces {} around the body. Option A is missing the 'function' keyword. Option B is a variable. Option D puts = in the wrong place.

exercises.title: Drop the function keyword
tasks: Drop the correct keyword into the blank to define a function called startGame.
exercises.hint: Every function definition starts with the same keyword — the word that tells JavaScript you are making a function.
exercises.explanation: 'function' is the keyword that tells JavaScript you are creating a reusable block of code. 'let' and 'const' are for variables. 'define' is not a JavaScript keyword at all.

exercises.title: Drop the full function signature
tasks: Drop all the parts into the correct order to define a function called showScore.
exercises.hint: A function definition has three parts on the first line: the keyword, the name with parentheses, and the opening curly brace.
exercises.explanation: The first line of a function is: function, then the name with parentheses (), then an opening curly brace {. The closing curly brace } goes at the very end to close the function body.

exercises.title: Defining vs calling
tasks: The code below defines a function. What needs to be added to actually run it?
exercises.hint: Calling a function uses the function name followed by something specific.

options.label: Nothing — it runs automatically when defined

exercises.explanation: The () at the end is what triggers execution. Without the (), you are just referencing the function — not running it. 'run' and 'call' are not JavaScript keywords.

exercises.title: Run your first function
tasks: Hit Run — nothing prints yet. The function is defined but never called.
Write greetPlayer(); on line 6. Hit Run.
You should see 'Welcome to Simon Says!' in the console.
Now call it again on line 7. You should see the message twice.
exercises.hint: To call a function write its name followed by (). Make sure you spell it exactly right.

steps.text: Hit Run — nothing prints yet. The function is defined but never called.
Write greetPlayer(); on line 6. Hit Run.
Call it again on line 7. You should see the message twice.
Same function, called twice, same result both times. That is reusability.

exercises.title: Call it three times
tasks: Write a function called showRound that logs 'Round starting...'.
Call it exactly three times below the definition.
You should see the message printed three times in the console.
Notice: you wrote the log once but it runs three times.
exercises.hint: Define the function first, then call it three times on separate lines: showRound(); showRound(); showRound();
exercises.starterCode:
// Define showRound here


// Call it three times below

steps.text: Write a function called showRound that logs 'Round starting...'.
Call it exactly three times. Hit Run.
You wrote the log once. It ran three times. That is the whole point of functions.

exercises.title: Function with a parameter
tasks: Fill in both blanks to complete this function. It should take a name and use it in the greeting.
exercises.hint: The parameter is declared inside the parentheses. Then you use that same name inside the function body.
exercises.explanation: A parameter is a variable that lives inside the parentheses. You give it a name — here 'name' — and then use that same name inside the function body. When you call greet('Jordan'), the word 'Jordan' gets stored in 'name' for that call.

exercises.title: Call a function with arguments
tasks: Hit Run — nothing prints yet.
Call lightUp with index 3. Hit Run.
Call it again with index 7. Then call it with index 0.
You should see three messages — each showing a different square number.
exercises.hint: To call with an argument: lightUp(3); The number goes inside the parentheses.

steps.text: Hit Run — nothing prints yet. The function exists but hasn't been called.
Call lightUp with index 3. Write lightUp(3); and hit Run.
Now call it with 7 and 0 as well. You should see three lines.
One function, three different inputs, three different outputs. This is how Simon Says lights up different squares each round.

exercises.title: Write startRound from scratch
tasks: Write a function called startRound.
Inside it, log two things: 'Picking squares...' and then 'Get ready!'.
Call it twice below the definition.
This is the first real function in the Simon Says game.
exercises.hint: Define startRound with function startRound() { ... }. Put two console.log lines inside. Then call it: startRound();
exercises.starterCode:
// Write startRound here




// Call it twice

steps.text: Write a function called startRound with two log lines inside. Hit Run.
Call startRound twice below the definition. Hit Run.
You just wrote startRound — a real function from the Simon Says game. Everything from here builds on this.
